note: After il faut que, French normally uses the subjonctif. Here, venir becomes que tu viennes.
exampleFrench: Il faut que tu viennes en classe à l'heure pour bien suivre la leçon.
exampleEnglish: You need to come to class on time to follow the lesson well.
imagePrompt: A student arriving on time for French class.

note: After je sais que, French uses the indicatif because the speaker presents the action as a fact: tu viens.
exampleFrench: Je sais que tu viens en classe tous les lundis.
exampleEnglish: I know that you come to class every Monday.
imagePrompt: A student attending class every Monday.

note: Expressions of emotion like je suis content que are followed by the subjonctif. The verb etre becomes que tu sois.
exampleFrench: Je suis content que tu sois plus à l'aise en français maintenant.
exampleEnglish: I am happy that you are more comfortable in French now.
imagePrompt: A French learner feeling more confident.

note: After je vois que, the speaker observes a real situation, so French uses the indicatif: tu es.
exampleFrench: Je vois que tu es plus confiant quand tu parles français.
exampleEnglish: I can see that you are more confident when you speak French.
imagePrompt: A teacher noticing a student's confidence.

note: After je doute que, French usually takes the subjonctif because the statement is uncertain: qu'elle ait.
exampleFrench: Je doute qu'elle ait toutes les réponses pour l'examen oral.
exampleEnglish: I doubt that she has all the answers for the oral exam.
imagePrompt: A student unsure before an oral exam.

note: After il est certain que, the information is presented as certain, so the indicatif is used: elle a.
exampleFrench: Il est certain qu'elle a le niveau pour réussir ce cours.
exampleEnglish: It is certain that she has the level needed to pass this course.
imagePrompt: A student clearly ready to pass a course.

note: After je veux que, French uses the subjonctif because it expresses will or desire: que nous fassions.
exampleFrench: Le professeur veut que nous fassions plus de conversations en français.
exampleEnglish: The teacher wants us to have more conversations in French.
imagePrompt: A French teacher encouraging more speaking practice.

note: After je remarque que, the sentence reports an observed fact, so the indicatif is natural: nous faisons.
exampleFrench: Je remarque que nous faisons moins d'erreurs qu'au début du semestre.
exampleEnglish: I notice that we make fewer mistakes than at the beginning of the semester.

note: After il est possible que, French often uses the subjonctif because the action is only possible, not certain: qu'ils puissent.
exampleFrench: Il est possible qu'ils puissent finir le projet avant vendredi.
exampleEnglish: It is possible that they can finish the project before Friday.
imagePrompt: Students trying to finish a project before Friday.

note: After je pense que, French usually uses the indicatif in affirmative statements: ils peuvent.
exampleFrench: Je pense qu'ils peuvent expliquer la règle en français maintenant.
exampleEnglish: I think they can explain the rule in French now.
imagePrompt: Students explaining a grammar rule in French.

note: Avant que is followed by the subjonctif because the action has not happened yet: que vous partiez.
exampleFrench: Je veux vous parler avant que vous partiez de l'école.
exampleEnglish: I want to talk to you before you leave school.
imagePrompt: A teacher speaking to students before they leave school.

note: When the speaker states a known plan or fact with je sais que, French uses the indicatif: vous partez.
exampleFrench: Je sais que vous partez demain matin pour votre stage.
exampleEnglish: I know that you are leaving tomorrow morning for your internship.
imagePrompt: Students leaving in the morning for an internship.
